name: Portugal
summary: Strong study-and-stay base with a real Bangladeshi/Muslim community, but the 2025-26 reforms gutted its headline advantage: citizenship now 10 years (not 5), AIMA is in deep backlog, and no long-term visa is processed in Dhaka.

links.title: AIMA - Agency for Integration, Migration and Asylum

job-market.summary: Growing Lisbon/Porto tech scene, English-friendly startups, but notably lower salaries (~€43k avg) than NW Europe.
job-market.reasoning: Lisbon and Porto are genuine tech hubs with international firms and English-speaking startups, giving both engineers realistic prospects. But average software-engineer pay (~€43k, seniors €55-58k) is well below Northern Europe, and sponsorship competes with a large applicant pool.
evidence: Average software-engineer salary ~€43,000/yr; seniors ~€57,800 in Lisbon (Glassdoor 2025).
Lisbon/Porto established as international tech hubs attracting startups and multinationals.
English widely used in tech firms; demand for developers/AI skills is rising.
Salaries materially lag NW Europe; cost-adjusted savings are moderate.

visa-access.summary: No long-term visa processing in Dhaka; BD applicants apply via Embassy of Portugal in New Delhi, plus severe AIMA backlog.
visa-access.reasoning: Bangladeshi nationals cannot obtain a national (D/student) visa in Dhaka - the honorary consulate handles no visas; long-term applications route to the Embassy of Portugal in New Delhi (travel/cost burden). Once in-country, AIMA's ~400k-case backlog and the April 2025 strict-complete-application rule add heavy delay and rejection risk.
evidence: Long-term (national D/student) visas for BD applicants are submitted at the Embassy of Portugal in New Delhi, India.
Dhaka VFS / French Embassy handle only short-stay Schengen, not national visas.
AIMA carried ~400,000 backlogged cases in 2025; permit waits of weeks to many months.
Since 28 Apr 2025 AIMA rejects any application missing a single document.

citizenship.summary: 2025-26 reform extended naturalisation from 5 to 10 years (BD not CPLP). Dual allowed, A2 Portuguese - but the clock is now long.
citizenship.reasoning: Portugal's famed 5-year route is gone: the reform (parliament Apr 2026, signed by the President 3 May 2026) sets 10 years for non-EU/non-CPLP nationals, and Bangladesh is not a CPLP country so the 7-year track does not apply. The residence clock starts only at permit issuance and student time counts at a reduced rate, pushing real timelines beyond 10 years; dual citizenship and modest A2 language remain positives.
evidence: Reform raises general naturalisation to 10 years (7 for EU/CPLP); BD nationals fall under 10 years.
Enacted: parliament approved Apr 2026, President signed 3 May 2026; effect on publication.
Dual citizenship permitted - no renunciation of Bangladeshi nationality required.
Residence counted only from permit issuance; student years count at a reduced rate.

links.title: The Portugal News - citizenship rules and timelines

post-study-work.summary: 12-month post-study job-seeker permit + student-to-work conversion via 20-day Green Route; full work rights but non-renewable.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of Portuguese universities get a 12-month post-study job-seeker residence permit with full work rights, and the 'Green Route' processes work-permit conversions in ~20 days. The window is non-renewable (must secure a job within 12 months) and the standalone job-seeker visa was tightened in Oct 2025, but the student-to-work path remains workable.
evidence: Post-study job-seeker residence permit: 12 months, full work rights, ~€13k subsistence proof.
Government 'Green Route' targets ~20-day processing for work-permit conversion.
Permit is non-renewable - a job must be secured within the 12 months.
Oct 2025: standalone Skilled Job Seeker visa now needs degree + 5 yrs experience.

links.title: AIMA - residence permits for work and study

spouse-family.summary: Reunified family gets work rights, BUT 2025 law makes sponsor wait ~2 years before bringing spouse - a real gap.
spouse-family.reasoning: Family reunification grants the spouse the right to work under the same conditions as the sponsor, which suits a two-engineer couple - but the 2025 immigration law generally requires the sponsor to hold a valid permit for ~2 years first (reducible to 15 months in some cohabitation cases). Best mitigated by both spouses enrolling as students so each holds an independent permit with work rights.
evidence: Reunified family members may live and work under the same conditions as the sponsor.
2025 law: sponsor must usually hold a permit ~2 years before reunification (15 months in some cases).
Workaround: both enrol as students, each holding an independent permit with study-period work rights.
Reunified family must attend Portuguese-language and civic-values training.

links.title: EU Immigration Portal - Family member in Portugal

msc-study.summary: Growing English MSc offer, low tuition (~€3-7k/yr non-EU), part-time student work, spouse can accompany/enrol.
msc-study.reasoning: Public universities offer an expanding set of English-taught Masters (incl. CS/data/AI) at low non-EU tuition of roughly €3,000-7,000/yr, with part-time work rights during study. The spouse - also an engineer - can enrol in parallel or accompany, making a both-students entry the cleanest dual-work setup; English-medium CS choice is still narrower than UK/Netherlands.
evidence: Non-EU Masters tuition typically ~€3,000-7,000/yr (e.g. U.Porto CS Master €3,750 for 2025/26).
Growing range of English-taught Masters, though some flagship CS courses remain Portuguese.
Students hold work rights for part-time/limited hours during studies.
Both spouses can enrol, each gaining an independent residence permit.

links.title: University of Porto - Faculty of Sciences

pr-pathway.summary: Permanent residence after ~5 years legal stay + A2 Portuguese, but AIMA backlog makes timing unpredictable.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Permanent residence (and EU long-term resident status) is reachable after 5 years of legal residence with A2 Portuguese, stable income and tax compliance - notably shorter than the new 10-year citizenship bar. The catch is AIMA processing: backlogs can stretch decisions to 1-2 years, eroding predictability for this couple.
evidence: PR requires 5 years legal residence, A2 Portuguese, stable income and tax/social-security compliance.
Student and work years of legal residence count toward the 5-year PR threshold.
AIMA decisions can take 1-2 years amid the national backlog.
PR clock is shorter and more attainable than the 10-year citizenship track.

income-cost.summary: Low historic cost of living offset by lower tech pay and fast-rising Lisbon rents - moderate net savings for two engineers.
income-cost.reasoning: Tech salaries are comfortably above the Portuguese median, so a dual-engineer household lives well, but rents have surged (Lisbon 1-bed centre ~€1,408/mo; metro rents +35% since 2020) while pay lags NW Europe. Result is moderate, not generous, savings - better than locals but below higher-paying EU destinations.
evidence: Lisbon 1-bed city-centre rent ~€1,408/mo; metro rents up >35% since 2020.
Average tech pay (~€3,690/mo) sits well above the general workforce.
National rents ~€16.8/m2 (Aug 2025), still climbing ~3.3% yoy.
Two tech incomes yield moderate savings; single income would be tight in Lisbon.

links.title: Numbeo - Cost of Living in Lisbon

healthcare.summary: Universal tax-funded SNS, low cost and good clinical quality, but long waits and GP shortages strain access.
healthcare.reasoning: Legal residents with a social-security number access the universal SNS at low or no cost, and clinical quality in major hospitals is well regarded. However, waiting lists are large (nearly 975k waiting for a first specialist consultation mid-2025) and 1.5m+ residents lack an assigned GP, so private top-up insurance is commonly used in Lisbon/Porto.
evidence: Universal SNS covers all legal residents with a social-security number.
~974,770 patients awaited a first hospital consultation by mid-2025.
Over 1.56m residents lacked an assigned family doctor in early 2025.
Portugal ranked 23rd in Numbeo's 2025 Health Care Index; private insurance is affordable.

culture-language.summary: High English use in cities/tech; daily life manageable in English, with A2 Portuguese needed for PR/citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: English is widely spoken in Lisbon/Porto, tech workplaces and universities, so day-to-day life and work are manageable without fluent Portuguese. Portuguese is a Romance language that is relatively learnable to the A2 level required for PR and citizenship, and the established South-Asian community further eases settling in.
evidence: High English proficiency in cities, tech firms and universities.
Only A2 Portuguese required for PR/citizenship - a modest, achievable bar.
Large Bangladeshi/South-Asian community supports a soft cultural landing.
Portuguese (Romance language) is comparatively approachable for A2 study.

safety-law.summary: Among the world's safest (7th on 2025 Global Peace Index); solid rule of law (WJP 29th), low violent crime.
safety-law.reasoning: Portugal ranks 7th on the 2025 Global Peace Index with low violent crime and high perceived safety, an important reassurance for a relocating couple. Rule of law is solid (WJP 2025 ~29th of 143) though not top-tier; petty theft in tourist areas is the main everyday concern.
evidence: 7th worldwide in the 2025 Global Peace Index.
WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: ~29th of 143 countries.
Low violent crime; ~83% feel safe walking alone at night (Gallup 2024).
Main risk is petty theft/pickpocketing in tourist zones.

politics.summary: Stable institutions but recent turmoil: 3rd election in 3 years, far-right Chega surging, 2025-26 immigration tightening.
politics.reasoning: Portugal's institutions are democratic and stable, but it has held three general elections in three years under fragile minority governments. Far-right Chega's 2025 breakthrough (now main opposition) has pushed immigration and nationality rules tighter - directly visible in the citizenship extension and family-reunification curbs - creating policy risk for migrants.
evidence: Third general election in three years; recurrent minority governments.
Far-right Chega surged to ~60 seats (May 2025), becoming main opposition.
2025-26 saw nationality extended to 10 years and family reunification curbed.
Immigration is a top political flashpoint, raising future-policy uncertainty.

links.title: Atlantic Council - Portugal's shift to the right

tax.summary: High top marginal IRS (48% + surcharges), but IFICI/NHR 2.0 offers 20% flat on income for qualifying tech roles.
tax.reasoning: Standard income tax is high - progressive to 48% above ~€81k plus solidarity surcharges - which weighs on dual high earners. The IFICI regime (NHR 2.0) gives a 20% flat rate on eligible professional income to qualifying high-skilled/tech workers not tax-resident in the prior 5 years, materially softening the burden if both qualify.
evidence: Top marginal IRS rate 48% above ~€81,199; +2.5-5% solidarity surcharge for high incomes.
IFICI (NHR 2.0): 20% flat on eligible Portuguese professional income.
IFICI needs no PT tax residence in prior 5 years + qualifying degree/role (CS/tech eligible).
Original NHR closed to new applicants; final transition window ended Mar 2025.

links.title: Autoridade Tributária e Aduaneira (Portuguese Tax Authority)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Established Bangladeshi/South-Asian hub in Lisbon (Martim Moniz), multiple mosques, halal food and services widespread.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Lisbon hosts a long-standing, visible Bangladeshi community centred on Martim Moniz/Rua do Benformoso, with Bangladeshi-run mosques, halal shops, remittance services and restaurants - a major comfort factor for this Muslim couple. The overall Muslim population is modest nationally but the South-Asian/Bangladeshi presence specifically is strong and growing.
evidence: Bangladeshi community concentrated in Lisbon's Martim Moniz ('Bengali Gali') and around Porto.
Several Bangladeshi-supervised mosques plus the Lisbon Central Mosque.
Rua do Benformoso lined with Bangladeshi restaurants, halal meat and money-transfer shops.
Community growing since the 1990s; widely documented diaspora presence.

links.title: The Daily Star - Bangladeshi immigrants in Lisbon
Comunidade Islâmica de Lisboa (Lisbon Central Mosque)
